metadata.title: Computer Use in Production: The $50 Screenshot Problem.

skin_warnings: B06: palette=claude but the outro is 'LogoOutro' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

props.topic: HUMANITARIANS AI · Computer Use in Production: The $50 Screenshot Problem.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The naive screenshot loop sends full-resolution images every step, running the token meter up fast. Resizing to 1568px width preserves the visual information at a fraction of the tokens. Pruning older screenshots and caching the unchanging system prompt cut the cost further. Batched tool calls and trajectory recording reduce round-trips and let you replay and audit failures.

artifactLines: The naive screenshot loop sends full-resolution images every step, running the token meter up fast.
Resizing to 1568px width preserves the visual information at a fraction of the tokens.
Pruning older screenshots and caching the unchanging system prompt cut the cost further.
Batched tool calls and trajectory recording reduce round-trips and let you replay and audit failures.

beats.narration_text: Here is why Computer Use in Production: The $50 Screenshot Problem. belongs in your working knowledge. The systems, policies, and tools shaped by it affect decisions at your level — whether or not you understand the mechanism. Knowing the underlying logic gives you the frame; missing it means reasoning from output with no access to the inputs.

artifactLines: The naive screenshot loop sends full-resolution images every step, running the token meter up fast.
Resizing to 1568px width preserves the visual information at a fraction of the tokens.
Pruning older screenshots and caching the unchanging system prompt cut the cost further.
Batched tool calls and trajectory recording reduce round-trips and let you replay and audit failures.

beats.narration_text: The fifty-dollar-screenshot framing made the token cost of the naive loop concrete for me, and the three fixes — resize to 1568, prune old screenshots, cache the system prompt — are things I could apply today. For a fifty-step web task, can you (1) estimate the token savings from each fix separately so I know which matters most; (2) explain the click-and-declare-expectation pattern versus click-wait-observe; (3) show what a single trajectory-recording event should log so I can replay a failure?

props.topic: HUMANITARIANS AI · BUILD WITH CLAUDE · ANTHROPIC
props.segment: Computer Use in Production: The $50 Screenshot Problem.
props.command: The fifty-dollar-screenshot framing made the token cost of the naive loop concrete for me, and the three fixes — resize to 1568, prune old screenshots, cache the system prompt — are things I could apply today. For a fifty-step web task, can you (1) estimate the token savings from each fix separately so I know which matters most; (2) explain the click-and-declare-expectation pattern versus click-wait-observe; (3) show what a single trajectory-recording event should log so I can replay a failure?

beats.narration_text: The naive loop is: take screenshot, send full-res image, get action, execute action, repeat. At 1920×1080 a single screenshot is eight megapixels. Uncompressed, that's thousands of tokens per step. Fifty steps into a task, you've sent fifty images. The token meter is running.

graphic.production_viz: Loop diagram: Screenshot -> Full-res -> Tokens -> Action, step counter, cost meter rising in terracotta

beats.narration_text: Three fixes address this. First: resize every screenshot to 1568px width before sending — same visual information, fraction of the tokens. Second: prune older screenshots from the context window; the model only needs recent state. Third: prompt caching on the system prompt — the instructions don't change per step, so cache them.

graphic.production_viz: Before/after token count bar — resize + prune dramatically reduces cost; cache shown separately

beats.narration_text: Batched tool calls: instead of click, wait, observe — you click and declare what you expect to see, then verify. Fewer round-trips. Trajectory recording logs every action as a structured event — click target, timestamp, screenshot delta — so you can replay failures and audit cost per step. The demo has none of this. The production agent has all of it.

graphic.production_viz: Two-column: naive (one call per action) vs batched (declare + verify), then trajectory log diagram

beats.narration_text: Ask Claude: 'Walk me through a production computer-use loop — image sizing, context pruning, caching strategy, and trajectory logging all included.' That question forces the full architecture into one place. What decisions does it surface that the demo skips? Run it. Kore, in for Humanitarians AI.
